ClinicalDocument.title: Continuity of Care Document - Sophia Thomas

value.displayName: for persistent vomiting to enable oral rehydration
if amoxicillin failure or recent antibiotic use

act.text: Well-child examination - 14 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with some red bumps on her chest and arms. They've been spreading throughout the day, and she says they're itchy. We gave her some calamine lotion and had her take a cool bath, but they keep getting worse. I'm worried it might be something contagious since her friend was sick last week, and I'm not sure if she needs antibiotics or anything."

Rash: Clinical presentation of rash with normal vital signs (HR 67) and unremarkable systemic exam (HEENT normocephalic, lungs CTAB) supports benign dermatologic process without evidence of systemic involvement or hemodynamic compromise.
- [other] Topical care as appropriate for rash type - [education] Return if rash spreads, becomes painful, or child develops fever
Well-child examination - 13 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning complaining that her stomach hurt, and then she threw up a couple times. Since then she's had diarrhea on and off—maybe 4 or 5 times today. She's not really eating anything, just sipping a little water and ginger ale. We gave her some Pepto-Bismol last night, but it doesn't seem to be helping much. I'm mainly worried because she looks pretty tired and I want to make sure she's not getting dehydrated."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent with viral gastroenteritis: mild dehydration evidenced by tachycardia (HR 108) and slightly dry mucous membranes, with otherwise unremarkable systemic exam (clear lungs, normal HEENT) supporting primary GI process without complications.

- [medication] Ondansetron 7mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 12 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning complaining that her head hurt and felt really hot. We took her temperature and it was 102, so we gave her ibuprofen and she seemed better for a little while, but then it came back up. She's been pretty tired all day, doesn't want to eat much, and keeps saying her whole body aches. We're just worried it might be something serious—she's never been sick like this before."

Fever: History and exam consistent w/ fever: Tachycardia (HR 104) reflects systemic response to elevated core temp; normal HEENT and clear lungs help exclude acute focal infection (otitis, pharyngitis, pneumonia) as primary etiology, suggesting viral syndrome or other systemic process.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

"She woke up yesterday morning complaining her ear hurt, and by afternoon her temperature was 102. She's been pretty miserable—won't eat much and keeps saying it throbs. We gave her ibuprofen and it helped a little, but this morning it was just as bad. I'm worried it's getting worse and want to make sure it doesn't turn into something more serious."

Acute Otitis Media: **Clinical Reasoning:**
History and exam findings are consistent with acute otitis media (AOM): fever (102.2°F), tachycardia (HR 115), and classic otoscopic findings of erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy, indicating middle ear effusion under pressure. Absence of respiratory symptoms (clear lungs) excludes concurrent URI complications and supports primary middle ear infection.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 3000mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 3000mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 390mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 11 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been complaining that her head and body ache. We gave her ibuprofen a couple times and it brings the fever down temporarily, but it keeps coming back. She's not eating much and just wants to lay around, which isn't like her. We're mainly worried because the fever hasn't gone away and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with normal vital signs (HR 76), clear lungs, and benign HEENT exam suggests acute viral illness without evidence of localized infection or respiratory compromise. Normal auscultatory findings and hemodynamic stability support uncomplicated febrile illness likely self-limited in nature.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and said her throat felt scratchy. By last night her temperature was up to 101 and a half, so we gave her some ibuprofen and she felt a little better. Today the fever came back and she's been blowing her nose constantly—just clear stuff though. She's not eating much and seems pretty tired, but she's still drinking. We're mainly worried because she has soccer tournament this weekend and want to make sure it's not something serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: **Clinical Reasoning:**
History and exam findings consistent with viral URI: clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, mild pharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear bilateral lung fields. Low-grade fever (101.5°F) and normal vital signs support uncomplicated upper respiratory tract involvement without evidence of bacterial superinfection or lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 500mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 340mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 10 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and said her throat felt scratchy. By last night she had a fever and was complaining that her head hurt, so we gave her some ibuprofen and that seemed to help a little. This morning the fever came back and she's got a lot of clear mucus coming out of her nose. She's not eating much and just wants to lay around, which isn't like her. We're mainly worried because she has soccer tryouts next week and want to make sure this clears up okay."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: fever, tachycardia, and clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa indicate acute viral rhinosinusitis; clear lung fields exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 440mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 290mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"Her left eye started getting red and watery yesterday morning. By this afternoon it was really swollen and had some yellow discharge, so I knew we needed to come in. We tried flushing it with warm water and kept her home from school today. I'm worried it might be something serious or that her sister will catch it."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings of conjunctival injection with purulent discharge and eyelid edema are consistent with acute conjunctivitis. Normal corneal exam and visual acuity exclude keratitis and more serious anterior segment pathology.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia

"She woke up yesterday morning complaining that her ear hurt, and by afternoon her temperature was up to 102. She's been pretty miserable—won't eat much and keeps saying it hurts when she swallows. I gave her some ibuprofen and a heating pad last night which helped a little, but this morning she still has the fever and keeps tugging at her ear. I'm worried it's gotten worse and want to make sure it doesn't turn into something more serious."

Acute Otitis Media: Clinical presentation consistent with AOM: fever (102.4°F), erythematous and bulging TM with absent light reflex, and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy indicate acute suppurative middle ear infection with effusion. Absence of respiratory findings excludes concurrent pneumonia.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 2600mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 2600mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 290mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 9 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

HPI: Sophia woke up yesterday morning complaining that her right ear hurt really bad. She's had a fever since then and has been pretty fussy—she won't eat much and just wants to lay around. We gave her ibuprofen and it helped a little bit, but the pain keeps coming back. We're worried it might be a bad infection since she's never had an ear infection before.

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with acute otitis media: fever, erythematous and bulging TM with loss of light reflex, and middle ear effusion on otoscopy are classic for AOM. Absence of respiratory findings rules out concurrent URI as primary etiology.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 2300mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 2300mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 260mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 8 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and said her throat felt scratchy. By afternoon she had a fever and has been pretty tired all day—just wants to lay on the couch. We gave her some children's ibuprofen and she's been drinking okay, but she's not interested in eating much. I'm mostly worried the fever will get worse or it'll turn into something more serious like an ear infection, since she had that twice last winter."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: fever, clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, and mild pharyngeal erythema without exudate support upper respiratory viral infection rather than bacterial pharyngitis. Clear lung fields and normal vital signs (HR 90) indicate no lower respiratory involvement or systemic toxicity.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 340mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 230mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She started throwing up yesterday morning around 8 AM, and she's had it maybe 5 or 6 times since then. She also has diarrhea and keeps saying her stomach hurts. We've been giving her small sips of water and ginger ale, but she won't really eat anything. I'm mostly worried about her getting dehydrated because she seems really tired and hasn't had a wet diaper since this morning."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: mild tachycardia (HR 104) suggests possible mild dehydration from GI losses, while normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude alternate diagnoses (meningitis, pneumonia) and indicate no respiratory compromise.

- [medication] Ondansetron 3mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Anaphylaxis due to food

"She woke up yesterday morning with a sore throat and has had a low fever on and off since then—we've been checking it with our thermometer at home. This morning she complained her head hurt and she's been pretty tired, just wanting to lay on the couch. She's not eating much, mostly just drinking water and some juice. We gave her children's ibuprofen yesterday which helped a little bit with the fever, but it keeps coming back. I'm mainly worried because she seems really uncomfortable and we want to make sure it's not strep or something that needs antibiotics."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 101) likely d/t systemic viral infection; normal HEENT & clear lungs b/l r/o bacterial otitis media, sinusitis, & lower respiratory tract involvement.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever—we checked it and it was 102.5. She's been complaining that her head and body ache, and she's just really tired and doesn't want to eat much. We gave her Tylenol and it comes down for a couple hours, but then it goes back up. This morning it was 103.8, so we figured we should bring her in. She's not acting like herself at all—just wants to lie on the couch." --- Key elements included: - Timeline: Onset yesterday morning - Specific fever reading (102.5°F, then 103.8°F) - Associated symptoms: headache, body aches, fatigue, anorexia - Home management: Tylenol given - Behavioral change noted - Reason for visit: Fever persistence and height

Fever: History and exam consistent w/ fever: Tachycardia (HR 101) supports systemic inflammatory response; normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude obvious focal infection sources (otitis, sinusitis, pneumonia), suggesting viral etiology or occult source requiring further workup.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 7 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 6 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever—I checked and it was 103. She's been pretty fussy and says her throat hurts when she swallows. She's not really eating, just picking at her food, but she's drinking okay. I gave her some ibuprofen and it brings the fever down for a couple hours, but then it comes back up. I'm mainly worried because the fever won't go away and she's just not acting like herself."

Fever: History and exam consistent w/ fever: Pt presents w/ elevated temp; vital signs show compensatory tachycardia (HR 88), while HEENT and lung exam are benign, suggesting fever likely viral in etiology w/o focal infection on initial assessment.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Finger laceration

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been complaining that her throat hurts. She's been pretty tired and won't eat much, though she's drinking okay. We gave her some children's Tylenol and a popsicle, but the fever keeps coming back. I'm mainly worried because she's acting pretty miserable and I want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 106) typical of systemic viral illness in pediatric pts; normal HEENT and clear lungs B/L rule out focal bacterial infection (otitis media, pneumonia), supporting benign viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been pretty miserable ever since. She's been complaining that her head and body hurt, and she's just lying around - not her normal self at all. We gave her some children's Tylenol this morning and it helped a little bit, but the fever came back. She's barely eating anything and we're worried because she's never been this sick before."

Influenza: Clinical presentation consistent w/ influenza: viral prodrome w/ systemic sx in 5yo; normal cardiopulmonary exam (HR 95, lungs CTA bilaterally) rules out secondary bacterial infection/pneumonia at this time, supporting uncomplicated influenza.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a really weird barky cough—like a seal sound. Her voice has gotten really hoarse since then, and the cough seems worse at night. We've been running the humidifier and giving her warm liquids, but it's not helping much. I'm mostly worried because she's having trouble breathing when she coughs and I want to make sure it doesn't get worse."

Croup: Clinical presentation consistent with croup: hoarse cry + inspiratory stridor are pathognomonic findings in laryngotracheobronchitis. Tachycardia (HR 133) and mild hypoxia (SpO2 94%) reflect respiratory distress, while clear lungs exclude lower airway involvement/pneumonia.

- [medication] Dexamethasone 10mg x1 dose x 1 days - [education] Single dose of dexamethasone is typically curative - [education] Cool mist or night air may provide temporary relief - [education] Keep child calm - crying worsens stridor - [education] Return IMMEDIATELY for stridor at rest, drooling, or respiratory distress

Mom reports that Sophia woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and mild cough. Over the past 24 hours, she's developed a low-grade fever (101.2°F at home) and seems more tired than usual, though she's still playing with toys intermittently. Mom gave her acetaminophen yesterday afternoon which helped temporarily, and she's been pushing fluids, but Sophia hasn't been eating much—mostly just crackers and juice. Mom's main concern is the fever and wanting to make sure it's nothing serious, as Sophia has been around several kids at school who've been sick recently.

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 110) typical of acute viral illness in pediatric pts; normal HEENT & clear lungs B/L exclude focal bacterial infection or pneumonia, supporting benign viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 5 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been sneezy all day. This morning she had a fever of about 101, and she's been complaining that her nose is stuffy and her throat hurts a little. We gave her some children's ibuprofen and she's been drinking okay, but she's definitely more tired than usual and doesn't want to eat much. We're mainly worried because she has school pictures tomorrow and wondering if this will get worse or if it's just a cold."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: fever, tachycardia, clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, and mild pharyngeal erythema without exudate support viral etiology; clear lungs exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 240mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 160mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been pretty congested ever since. We noticed she's been sneezing a lot and her nose won't stop running—it's clear stuff. She's eating okay but not as much as usual, and she seems a little fussy and tired. We gave her some saline drops and kept her home from preschool today, but we wanted to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: History and exam consistent w/ viral URI: clear rhinorrhea and mild erythema of nasal mucosa/oropharynx without exudate or systemic toxicity; tachycardia (HR 115) likely d/t fever/viral illness; clear lungs exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 240mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 160mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She woke up this morning with a really hoarse, raspy voice—almost like she can't talk right. Yesterday she had a runny nose and seemed a little tired, but this barking cough started last night and has gotten worse. We've been giving her warm liquids and ran the humidifier in her room, but she's still having trouble with the cough, especially at night. We're worried because she sounds so sick and we're not sure if she needs medicine or if it will go away on its own."

Croup: Clinical presentation consistent with croup: hoarse voice/cry reflects laryngeal inflammation from viral etiology, while clear lungs and absence of wheezes exclude lower airway involvement or alternative diagnoses (e.g., epiglottitis, asthma). Mild tachycardia (HR 100) is nonspecific but expected with acute viral illness.

- [medication] Dexamethasone 9mg x1 dose x 1 days - [education] Single dose of dexamethasone is typically curative - [education] Cool mist or night air may provide temporary relief - [education] Keep child calm - crying worsens stridor - [education] Return IMMEDIATELY for stridor at rest, drooling, or respiratory distress
Well-child examination - 4 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been really fussy ever since. She's had a runny nose and a little cough, and she won't eat much—just wants her juice. We gave her some Tylenol and she felt a little better for a bit, but the fever keeps coming back. I'm mostly worried because she's not acting like herself and I want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 111) suggests systemic infection/inflammatory response; normal HEENT & clear lungs B/L exclude localized bacterial infection (otitis, pneumonia), supporting viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 3 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She had a scraped knee about a week ago from falling at the park, and it seemed to be healing fine. But then yesterday morning I noticed some crusty, honey-colored stuff on it that wasn't there before, and now there's a couple more spots showing up on her shin. I've been keeping it clean and putting antibiotic ointment on it, but it just keeps spreading. She's been a little fussier than usual and doesn't want me to touch the areas because they seem sore. I'm worried it's getting infected and I want to make sure it doesn't get worse."

Impetigo: Clinical presentation consistent w/ impetigo: localized pyoderma w/ characteristic lesions (pustules/honey-crusted erosions) on exposed surfaces. Vitals show mild tachypnea (RR 25) likely 2° to discomfort/fever, but lungs clear & no systemic toxicity evident, suggesting superficial skin infection w/o deep tissue involvement or systemic complications.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She started throwing up yesterday morning, maybe around 9 or 10. At first we thought it was just one time, but then she kept doing it every hour or so. She's had diarrhea too—really loose, greenish stools. We've been trying to give her small sips of water and some crackers, but she won't really eat anything. She seems pretty fussy and tired, and I'm worried she's not getting enough fluids. She hasn't had a wet diaper since this morning." --- Key elements included: - Clear timeline (yesterday morning) - Symptom progression (repeated vomiting, diarrhea) - Home management attempted (fluids, crackers) - Observable behavioral changes (fussy, tired) - Parent's main concern (dehydration/fluid intake) - Clinical clue supporting exam findings (decreased wet diapers → likely some dehydration correlating with dry mucous membranes)

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent with viral gastroenteritis: tachycardia (HR 118) and tachypnea (RR 27) reflect compensatory response to likely mild dehydration evidenced by slightly dry mucous membranes, while clear lungs and normal HEENT exam exclude alternative diagnoses (pneumonia, meningitis).

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been really fussy ever since. We noticed she has a runny nose and a little cough, and she won't eat much—just some crackers and juice. We've been giving her Tylenol every few hours and it brings the fever down for a bit, but it keeps coming back. We're mainly worried because she's not herself and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 121) reflects systemic viral illness in toddler, while normal HEENT and clear lungs bilaterally exclude focal bacterial infection or pneumonia.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and low-grade fever around 101. Today she's been more fussy than usual, won't eat much, and has had some loose stools. We gave her children's Tylenol for the fever and she's been drinking okay with water and juice. We're mainly worried because she's just not acting like herself and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: History and exam consistent w/ viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 101) appropriate for age/fever, normal HEENT and lungs r/o bacterial infection or pneumonia. No focal findings to suggest alternative diagnosis.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with kind of a runny nose and was a little fussy, but nothing too bad. Then last night she developed this really weird barky cough—it sounds like a seal—and we got pretty scared. She's been running a low fever since this morning and the cough keeps getting worse, especially when she cries. We tried giving her some cool air from the bathroom and some water, but she won't really eat anything. We're worried about her breathing and want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Croup: Clinical presentation consistent with croup: inspiratory stridor with fever and tachypnea (RR 29) in 2 yo female. Absence of wheezes and clear lung fields rule out lower airway involvement, supporting laryngotracheobronchitis dx; stridor worsening with agitation typical of croup pathophysiology.

- [medication] Dexamethasone 7mg x1 dose x 1 days - [education] Single dose of dexamethasone is typically curative - [education] Cool mist or night air may provide temporary relief - [education] Keep child calm - crying worsens stridor - [education] Return IMMEDIATELY for stridor at rest, drooling, or respiratory distress
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started throwing up yesterday morning, maybe around 10 o'clock. At first we thought it was just one time, but then she kept doing it every hour or so. She's also had diarrhea—like 4 or 5 times today—and she's been really fussy and won't eat anything. We've been trying to give her small sips of water and Pedialyte, but I'm worried she's not getting enough in her and she seems pretty tired."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: tachycardia (HR 116) suggests possible mild dehydration from fluid losses, while normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude alternative diagnoses (meningitis, pneumonia) and support primary GI etiology.

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 1 year 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up fussy yesterday morning and felt warm, so we took her temperature and it was 102. She's had a runny nose and cough since then, and she's been pretty clingy—just wants to be held. She's not eating much, just picking at her food, but she's drinking her milk okay. We gave her some children's ibuprofen last night and it brought the fever down a bit, but it came back up this morning. We're mainly worried because she seems uncomfortable and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 104) and tachypnea (RR 27) suggest systemic viral illness, while normal HEENT and clear bilateral lung fields exclude bacterial superinfection or pneumonia at this time.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 1 year 3 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Mother reports that Sophia developed a fever yesterday afternoon. She's been fussy and won't eat her usual meals, though she's still drinking from her sippy cup. Mom checked her temperature this morning and it was 102.5°F, so she gave her children's acetaminophen which brought it down temporarily. She's concerned because Sophia hasn't had a fever like this before and wants to make sure it's nothing serious.

Fever: Clinical presentation consistent w/ fever: elevated HR (104) and RR (26) reflect appropriate physiologic response to pyrexia in toddler. Normal HEENT and clear lungs on exam help exclude acute URTI/pneumonia as primary etiology, suggesting systemic viral illness or occult infection requiring further workup (labs, UA, imaging as indicated).
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 1 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been really fussy ever since. She's been pulling at her right ear a lot and won't eat much—just taking a little bit of her bottle. We gave her some Tylenol this morning and it brought the fever down a little, but she's still not acting like herself. We're worried it might be an ear infection because she had one a couple months ago."

Acute Otitis Media: Clinical presentation consistent w/ AOM: fever (102.1°F), tachycardia (HR 118), tachypnea (RR 32), and decreased TM mobility on pneumatic otoscopy are classic findings. Absence of pulmonary findings excludes concurrent pneumonia, supporting isolated AOM dx.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 750mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 750mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 80mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 9 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started having diarrhea yesterday morning—really loose, watery stools maybe 4 or 5 times. Then this morning she had a fever and threw up twice after her bottle. She's been really fussy and just wants to sleep, and she's not interested in eating like she normally does. We've been giving her small amounts of water and pedialyte, but we're worried she's getting dehydrated because her lips seem dry and she hasn't had a wet diaper since last night."

Viral Gastroenteritis: History and exam consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: fever, tachycardia (HR 127), tachypnea (RR 27), and mild dehydration (dry mucous membranes) support acute infectious gastroenteritis; normal lung exam and HEENT findings exclude alternative diagnoses (pneumonia, meningitis).

- [medication] Ondansetron 1mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill

"She started getting a runny nose yesterday morning, and today it's gotten worse. She's been pretty fussy and doesn't seem as interested in her bottles—she'll take a few sips and then pulls away. We've been wiping her nose and using the bulb syringe to help her breathe better, but we're worried because she seems uncomfortable and we're not sure if she's getting enough to eat."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lung fields. Vital signs show mild tachycardia (HR 127) and tachypnea (RR 34) typical of viral URI in infants, with no evidence of lower respiratory involvement or bacterial superinfection.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 110mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 70mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She's been fussy and running a fever since yesterday afternoon. We took her temperature this morning and it was 101.2. She's not eating as much as usual—only taking about half her normal bottle—and she's been sleeping more than normal. We gave her some infant acetaminophen last night and it helped a little, but she's still not herself. We're worried because she's so young and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 142) and tachypnea (RR 31) suggest systemic viral infection with compensatory response, while clear lungs and benign HEENT exam exclude bacterial pneumonia or focal infection.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She's had a runny nose for about 2 days now—it started with just some sniffles, but yesterday it got worse with lots of clear stuff coming out. She's been a little fussy and doesn't seem as interested in her bottle as usual. We've been using the saline drops and a humidifier at night, but we're worried because she sounds congested and we want to make sure it's not something more serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear bilateral lung fields support upper respiratory involvement without evidence of lower respiratory tract disease or bacterial superinfection. Mild tachycardia (HR 132) and tachypnea (RR 33) are expected compensatory responses in a 4-month-old with acute viral illness.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 90mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 4 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 2 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - newborn
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Healthy newborn
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

tr.td: No developmental concerns at this time
Concerns: poor balance, limited problem-solving; Discussed concerns with family. Will monitor at next visit.
No developmental concerns at this time
No developmental concerns at this time
No developmental concerns at this time
